cards.concept: Why use standard library algorithms instead of custom loops
cards.explanation:
Benefits include:
  • Less error-prone (well-tested, debugged)
  • More readable and expressive
  • More efficient (optimized implementations)
  • Work with all container types via iterators
  • Easier to parallelize in future
examples: // vs writing custom loop with bugs

cards.text: Standard library algorithms are found in the {{c1::<algorithm>}} header and typically work with {{c2::iterators}} rather than containers directly.
To find a value in a container, use {{c1::std::find(std::begin(container), std::end(container), value)}} which returns an {{c2::iterator}}.

cards.question: What does this code check?
cards.answer: Checks if the search value was NOT found in the array.
cards.explanation: std::find returns std::end() if the value is not found. Comparing the result to end() tells us if the search failed.

cards.front: What does std::find return if the value is found?
cards.back: An iterator pointing to the first element matching the value.

cards.front: What does std::find return if the value is not found?
cards.back: An iterator equal to the end iterator passed as the second parameter (std::end()).

cards.text: To count occurrences of a value in a container, use {{c1::std::count(std::begin(container), std::end(container), value)}}.
To sort in descending order with std::sort, pass {{c1::std::greater}} as the third argument (comparison function).

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.answer: Sorts the array in descending order (largest to smallest).
cards.explanation: std::greater is a comparison function that reverses the default ascending order.

cards.text: To apply a function to each element in a range, use {{c1::std::for_each(std::begin(container), std::end(container), function)}}.

cards.front: Why do standard library algorithms take two iterators (begin and end) instead of just a container?
cards.back:
This allows algorithms to work on:
  • Part of a container (subrange)
  • Any container type
  • Custom ranges
  • Even arrays and built-in types
Maximum flexibility.
Algorithm that returns an iterator to the smallest element in a range
